coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of a plasmonic CuNi bimetal modified with carbon quantum dots as a non-semiconductor-driven photocatalyst for effective water splitting
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Piyong
Zeng, Gongchang
Song, Ting
Huang, Shaobin
Wang, Tingting
Zeng, Heping
coredata.dc:description: In artificial photocatalysis, attention is focused mainly on semiconductor photocatalysts, due to their long research history and excellent photocatalytic performance. Here, we demonstrate that dendrite-like plasmonic CuNi bimetal, used as a nonsemiconductor photocatalyst, produces electrons for photocatalytic water splitting by the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of the CuNi bimetal. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), by virtue of their outstanding charge transfer properties, serve as a co-catalyst that can facilitate the transfer of the photoinduced charge. The photocatalytic water splitting rate of the CuNi bimetal was significantly improved after modification with CQDs, and the highest hydrogen evolution rate was 55.19 μmol g−1h−1, with an oxygen evolution rate of 27.51 μmol g−1h−1, when the content of CQDs in the photocatalyst was 3 wt%. X-ray diffraction and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy characterization reveal that the structure and state of the element in the photocatalyst remain unchanged after four consecutive cycles of, cumulatively, 20 h. Our findings may help in the development of a non-semiconductor-driven photocatalytic system for application in the photocatalytic field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nonsemiconductor
CuNi bimetal
Surface plasmon resonance
Photocatalytic water splitting

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21571064 , 21371060 ), the Ph.D. Start-up Fund of the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province ( 2008A030310362 ), and the research fund of the Key Laboratory of Fuel Cell Technology of Guangdong Province for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology of CuNi5:5/CQDs3 wt.%: (a, b, c) SEM images, (d) EDX spectrum, (e) elemental mapping pattern, (f, g) TEM images, and (h) HRTEM image.

(a) XRD patterns of the photocatalysts and CQDs (inset); (b) XRD patterns of the photocatalysts with an enlarged scale from 42° to 46°; (c) UV–vis absorbance spectra of the photocatalysts; (d) Fourier transform infrared spectra of CuNi5:5/CQDs3 wt.%.
XPS measurements of CuNi5:5/CQDs3 wt.%. (a) Survey spectrum, (b) high-resolution C1s, (c) Cu2p and Cu LMM (inset), and (d) Ni2p.

(a) Gas evolution rates of the CuNi bimetal with different mole ratios, (b) gas evolution rates of CuNi5:5 modified with different amounts of CQDs, and (c) stability measurements of the photocatalytic water splitting of CuNi5:5/CQDs3 wt.% (irradiation time 20 h).
(a) Transient photocurrent responses and (b) steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectra of pure Cu, CuNi5:5, and CuNi5:5/CQDs3 wt.%.
Schematic of the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction on CuNi5:5/CQDs3 wt.%.
Rates of H2 evolution with different photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of a plasmonic CuNi bimetal modified with carbon quantum dots as a non-semiconductor-driven photocatalyst for effective water splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Engineering of Guangdong Province, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510641, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Engineering of Guangdong Province
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment and Energy, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510641, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In artificial photocatalysis, attention is focused mainly on semiconductor photocatalysts, due to their long research history and excellent photocatalytic performance. Here, we demonstrate that dendrite-like plasmonic CuNi bimetal, used as a nonsemiconductor photocatalyst, produces electrons for photocatalytic water splitting by the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of the CuNi bimetal. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), by virtue of their outstanding charge transfer properties, serve as a co-catalyst that can facilitate the transfer of the photoinduced charge. The photocatalytic water splitting rate of the CuNi bimetal was significantly improved after modification with CQDs, and the highest hydrogen evolution rate was 55.19 μmol g−1h−1, with an oxygen evolution rate of 27.51 μmol g−1h−1, when the content of CQDs in the photocatalyst was 3 wt%. X-ray diffraction and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy characterization reveal that the structure and state of the element in the photocatalyst remain unchanged after four consecutive cycles of, cumulatively, 20 h. Our findings may help in the development of a non-semiconductor-driven photocatalytic system for application in the photocatalytic field.

ce:section.ce:para: Increasing attention is being drawn to developing efficient photocatalysts for converting solar energy into hydrogen fuels at low cost and energy consumption [1–3]. Semiconductors such as TiO2 [3–7] and g-C3N4 [8–12] have been widely explored in photocatalytic fields, but photocatalytic activity studies of nonsemiconductors are still scarce. To realize applications of nonsemiconductors in photocatalytic fields, plasmonic Cu has been studied and used for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution due to its surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect. The SPR effect can be initiated via the strong coupling of metal nanoparticles with light at specific photon energies because of the optical excitation of the collective electronic resonances of the metal [13–15]. Alternatively, the intrinsic activity of Cu sites can be changed by alloying Cu with other metals to form a bimetal, which can tune the surface d state due to ligand and strain effects, improving photocatalytic activities [16–19].

Nickel (Ni), which is an abundant and stable metal on Earth, has excellent photoabsorption properties that span the UV–visible and near-infrared regions of the optical spectrum. Ni-participant bimetals have been investigated in the catalytic field, and a number of methods have been used to improve their catalytic performance, such as core–shell [20] and geometric structures [21] and control of the crystal orientation [22]. A multicomponent configuration not only allows integration of the characteristics of the individual metal components but also enables a unique synergistic effect [23,24]. In a previous report [25], the dendritic structures of a Ni electrocatalyst demonstrated the highest catalytic activity due to geometric and electronic effects induced by a high population of (1 1 1) facets. The dendritic geometrical structures of a CuNi bimetal were not reported for photocatalytic water splitting, but excellent photocatalytic performance was expected.

Modification with carbon material is known to be a feasible method for tuning charge separation and transfer and for enhancing sunlight absorption for photocatalysis. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), an important carbon material, have attracted much scientific interest in the field of photocatalysis due to their excellent photoinduced charge transfer properties [26]. In many photocatalytic systems, CQDs act as an indispensable component for promoting photocatalytic activity, as in g-C3N4 [27], TiO2 [28], WO3 [29], and Bi2WO6 [30]. On the basis of this knowledge, we speculated that if the charge is rapidly transported away from the CuNi bimetal to an electron reservoir, CQDs, then the recombination of electron–hole pairs can be suppressed successfully in the CuNi bimetal. This can reasonably be expected to endow CuNi/CQDs with higher photocatalytic water-splitting activity for CuNi alone.

Here, we demonstrate that a dendrite-like plasmonic CuNi bimetal combined with CQDs is a highly efficient non-semiconductor-driven photocatalyst for water splitting under solar irradiation. The reaction can be enabled by photoinduced electrons with surface plasmon decay from the CuNi bimetal. To increase H2 and O2 evolution, CQDs were supported on the CuNi bimetal as a co-catalyst, ensuring that electron–hole pairs were sufficiently separated. Furthermore, the photocatalyst exhibited excellent stability after repeated cycling for time periods of 5 h under sunlight. The presented photocatalytic system may enhance the applicability and accessibility of the nonsemiconductor and plasmon fields.

All reagents are analytical grade and used without further purification. Copper chloride dihydrate (CuCl2·2H2O) (AR), nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O) (AR), ethylene diamine (AR), citric acid (AR), sodium hydroxide, absolute ethanol, and sodium hypophosphite (AR) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., China. Deionized water was used in all experiments.

The synthesis of the CuNi bimetal and the CQDs is described in the Supporting Information, and CuNi bimetal samples with different mole proportions of Cu and Ni are denoted as Cu, Ni, CuNi7:3, CuNi6:4, CuNi5:5, CuNi4:6, and CuNi3:7. CuNi/CQDs were prepared by the hydrothermal method. CuNi5:5 (20 mg) was dispersed into a mixed solution of 2 mL of absolute ethanol and 10 mL of distilled water. Then 1, 2, 4, 6, or 8 mL of a 0.1 g L−1 CQD solution was added to the mixed solution to realize 0.5, 1, 2, 3, or 4 wt% of CQDs in the CuNi5:5/CQDs. A homogeneous suspension was achieved after 0.5 h stirring and transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave with capacity 25 mL. These Teflon-lined autoclaves were sealed and heated to 120 °C at a rate of 1 °C/min and maintained for 4 h. The CuNi5:5/CQD photocatalysts were collected by centrifugation after cooling naturally to room temperature and washed three times each with absolute ethanol and distilled water. Finally, the products were dried under vacuum for 5 h at 50 °C. A schematic for the synthetic process of the photocatalysts is shown in Scheme 1 . The instruments used for characterization are described in the Supporting Information.

Photocatalytic water splitting measurements were performed in water under sunlight irradiation without a sacrificial agent. Typically, 5 mg of catalyst and 70 mL of deionized water were added to a Pyrex glass reaction cell with a 300 mL capacity, which was completely degassed approximately 0.5 h before irradiation. The light-irradiation experiments were carried out using a 300 W Xe lamp (PLS-SXE300CUV, Perfectlight Co., Beijing) without any filters to simulate solar irradiation as it reaches the earth’s surface (AM 1.5G). The amounts of H2 and O2 were determined by an online gas chromatograph (GC-7900, Tianmei, 5 Å molecular sieve column) with argon as the carrier gas. The temperature was maintained at 278 K by cool flowing water while a photocatalytic reaction was performed, which eliminated the influence of thermal reactions. The hydrogen evolution rate of each photocatalyst was tested three times.

A standard three-electrode setup was used with indium tin oxide (ITO)-coated glass as the working electrode, Pt foil as the counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. The working electrode was ITO-coated glass with a deposited photocatalyst (with an area of 1 cm2 and 5 mg of photocatalyst in 2 mL of ethanol). The transient photocurrent responses of the photocatalysts were carried out on a CHI 660D electrochemical station (Shanghai Chenhua, China) in 1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte under irradiation from a 300 W Xe lamp with on–off cycling by manual chopping.

The quantum efficiency is calculated from the equation
(1)
Q E =
2 × t h e
n u m b e r
o f
e v o l v e d
H 2
m o l e c u l e s
t h e
n u m b e r
o f
i n c i d e n t
p h o t o n s
( N )
× 100 %
N is determined by the equation
(2)
N =
E λ
hc
where E is the average intensity of irradiation, which is determined by an ILT 950 spectroradiometer (International Light Technologies), λ is wavelength, h is Planck’s constant, and c is the speed of light.

Fig. 1 b shows a low-magnification scanning electron micrograph (SEM) of the CuNi5:5/CQDs3 wt.% photocatalysts, which exhibit characteristic dendrite like microstructures. The length and width of the dendrite photocatalysts are approximately 3 and 2 μm, respectively. High-magnification SEM images of branches (Fig. 1a, c) reveal that dendritelike photocatalysts are composed of smaller branch structures that are on the nanoscale. The elemental compositions were mapped in Fig. 1d using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) analysis; C, O, Cu, and Ni were detected. Elemental mapping analysis of the SEM image displays the homogeneous dispersion of the Cu and Ni species within CuNi5:5/CQDs3 wt.% (Fig. 1e), illustrating that an evenly intermixed CuNi bimetal is achieved with high quality by the hydrothermal method rather than physically mixed phases of Cu and Ni. A transmission electron micrograph (TEM) of the as-synthesized CuNi5:5/CQDs3 wt.% also displays a dendrite like microstructure (Fig. 1f), which is consistent with the SEM images. The CQDs are not detectable by SEM or low-magnification TEM, because they are very small. A TEM image and the size distribution of the CQDs are given in Fig. S1 in the Supporting Information; the average particle size is approximately 4.5 nm. CQDs can be observed by TEM in Fig. 1g, and conjoined structures were formed between the CQDs and the CuNi bimetal. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) analysis revealed that the substrate material was the CuNi bimetal, with a (1 1 1) planar spacing of 0.206 nm, and the small particles were CQDs, with a lattice fringe of 0.32 nm corresponding to the (2 0 0) crystalline face (Fig. 1h).

Fig. 2 a shows the XRD patterns of the photocatalysts and CQDs. Characteristic patterns at 2θ = 43.2°, 50.4°, and 74.1° correspond to the (1 1 1), (2 0 0), and (2 2 0), respectively, of the Cu atomic plane (JCPDS No. 04-0836). The diffraction lines at 2θ = 44.4°, 51.8°, and 76.4° correspond to the (1 1 1), (2 0 0), and (2 2 0), respectively, of the Ni atomic plane (JCPDS No. 04-0850). Most importantly, the XRD pattern of the CuNi bimetal exhibits new diffraction lines that are located between the standard Cu and Ni diffraction lines and can be seen in Fig. 2b, implying that the mutual insertion of Cu and Ni atoms was formed rather than segregated [31]. These results are also consistent with the PDF card of the CuNi bimetal (JCPDS No. 65-7246) and the HRTEM image in Fig. 1h. Furthermore, XRD patterns of the CuNi bimetal with different mole ratios are displayed in Fig. S2. The patterns shift slightly to higher angles with increasing Ni content, demonstrating that the CuNi bimetal is formed at different mole ratios. A significant characteristic pattern at 21.4° is observed in the Fig. 2a inset corresponding, to the (0 0 2) plane of the CQDs [32]. The XRD pattern of CuNi5:5/CQDs3 wt.% exhibits the diffraction lines of the CuNi bimetal but hardly any of the patterns of the CQDs. The reasons that the diffraction line disappears for CQDs are probably their low concentration and low crystallinity [33]. In addition, diffraction lines of oxide impurities were not detected in these XRD patterns, suggesting a high purity for the photocatalysts. Furthermore, the XRD patterns of CuNi5:5 modified with different amounts of CQDs were also investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. S3. All the patterns are similar, and no patterns for CQDs are observed, due to the low concentration and low crystallinity of the CQDs.

The optical properties of the photocatalysts were examined, and the absorbance spectra are shown in Fig. 2c. An evident surface plasmon resonance (SPR) absorption band at a wavelength of 600 nm is observed for Cu and the CuNi5:5/CQDs photocatalysts due to the SPR effect of the Cu component [14,15]. Pure Ni exhibits a strong absorption over the whole region from the ultraviolet to the near-infrared. Compared to the pure Cu phase, an enhancement of the absorption was observed for the CuNi bimetal due to the contribution of the Ni component, especially in the longer-wavelength region, indicating that it can improve the absorption and utilization of sunlight. Furthermore, CuNi5:5/CQDs photocatalysts with different numbers of CQDs display similar absorption curves because of the small amount and weak absorption at wavelengths longer than 400 nm for the CQDs. It is important to note that the CuNi bimetal and the CuNi5:5/CQDs inherit the typical SPR absorption band of the Cu component, and this property is essential for plasmonic photocatalysis. In addition, the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum of the CQDs is shown in Fig. S4, and a relatively weak absorption band was observed. More details on CQDs and the diffuse reflectance spectrum are given in Supporting Information.

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of CuNi5:5/CQDs3 wt.% is displayed in Fig. 2d. The absorption band at 1654 cm−1 confirms the existence of carbonyl (CO) groups, while the absorption band at 3434 cm−1 corresponds to the OH stretching mode [34]. These absorption bands belong to surface functional groups (carboxylic and hydroxy groups) on the CQDs, which suggests that CQDs have reasonable hydrophilic properties and dispersibility in water. The strong absorption band at 1557 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of the amide linkage [35], confirming that N-doped CQDs incorporate many of the desired amino groups. The absorption bands at 1250–1000 cm−1 are assigned to the CC, CN, and CC groups in CQDs [36,37]. No absorption band for the CuNi bimetal is detected, because it is inactive in FTIR measurements. Furthermore, the Raman spectra of CuNi5:5/CQDs3 wt.% and CQDs are supplied in Fig. S5, and their discussion is included in the Supporting Information.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to investigate the subsurface and surface chemical states of CuNi5:5/CQDs3 wt.%. The binding energies obtained in the XPS analysis were corrected by referencing C1s to 284.6 eV with specimen charging. Fig. 3 a shows the survey XPS scan, confirming that the photocatalyst mainly contained C, N, O, Cu, and Ni. The high-resolution C1s spectrum in Fig. 3b exhibits four peaks at 284.6, 285.3, 286.2, and 288.2 eV, which are associated with CC/CC, CN, CO, and CO [38,39]. A satellite peak is not observed in the Cu2p spectrum (Fig. 3c), suggesting that Cu2+ is not present. The two peaks at binding energies of 932.4 and 952.6 eV are attributed to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, respectively, which can be assigned to Cu0 or Cu1+ [15]. Considering that Cu0 and Cu1+ have almost the same binding energy for Cu2p, further examination should be carried out to distinguish Cu0 and Cu1+. Auger electron spectroscopy of the sample is supplied in the Fig. 3c inset, and a peak at a kinetic energy of 918.6 eV, corresponding to Cu0, was detected, revealing that Cu2+ is successfully reduced to Cu0 in the hydrothermal method [40]. The presence of Ni was also detected by XPS, which displays two 2p peaks corresponding to the 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 for Ni(OH)2 at binding energies of 873.2 and 855.5 eV, two peaks at binding energies of 861.1 and 879.7 eV assigned to shake-up satellites, and two 2p peaks corresponding to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of Ni0 at binding energies of 852.3 and 869.8 eV [23,41]. This phenomenon suggests that Ni(OH)2 is located on the surface of the bimetal, in addition to CuNi, and hydroxylation is a common phenomenon for nanoscale materials [42]. It is worth noting that hydroxylation is not observed in the XRD pattern due to its low presence on the surface of the photocatalyst. XRD detects the overall phase, while XPS is a surface-level analysis 3–5 nm in depth. A high-resolution peak of O1s is presented in Fig. S6.

The photocatalytic water-splitting performance of the photocatalysts was evaluated under sunlight in a closed gas circulation system. Fig. 4 a shows the photocatalytic gas evolution rate of the CuNi bimetal at different mole ratios. It demonstrates that the CuNi5:5 sample obtains the highest H2 and O2 evolution rates of 15.33 and 7.52 μmol g−1h−1 (stoichiometry ratio of H2 and O2 approximately 2:1), respectively. Therefore, the optimized mole ratio is 5:5 in a bimetal system for photocatalysis. For pure Cu with the same synthetic process, a lower gas evolution rate was detected (6.24 and 3.11 μmol g−1h−1 for the H2 and O2 evolution rate) after the same light irradiation. No gas evolution was detected for Ni alone, however, suggesting that photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst originates from the Cu component due to its SPR effect. The combination of Cu with Ni offers a higher photocatalytic gas evolution rate than for pure Cu, due to the synergistic effect between Cu and Ni [23]. The photocatalytic effect of modification of the CuNi5:5 bimetal with CQDs in water splitting performance is also investigated. As shown in Fig. 4b, the hybrid CuNi5:5/CQDs containing a 3% mass proportion of CQDs exhibits the best performance, with H2 and O2 evolution rates of 55.19 and 27.15 μmol g−1h−1. The role of the CQDs is as an electron acceptor for photogenerated electron transfer, which can enhance photocatalytic activity. To check if the evolved gas is truly from water, we carried out isotopic experiments with H2 18O and D2O under identical photocatalytic reaction conditions. The resulting photocatalytic products were detected with the help of gas chromatography-mass spectrometry. It is clear that the detected isotopic peak with m/z = 4 is attributed to D2 in D2O. The peak with m/z = 36 is assigned to 18O2 in H2 18O. These results demonstrate that the evolved gas is from water.

The durability of the photocatalyst during photocatalysis was measured, and the performance was stable without apparent decay over four cycling tests (Fig. 4c). After irradiation with sunlight for 20 h, the gas evolution rate of CuNi5:5/CQDs3 wt.% decreases slightly. The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate decreased from 55.19 to 50.32 μmol g−1h−1, and still maintained 90% photocatalytic activity. XPS of Cu2p and Cu LMM (Fig. S7) and XRD patterns (Fig. S8) confirm that chemical states and crystal phase remain unchanged after four photocatalytic reaction cycles. Surface elemental compositions were also determined by XPS after the stability measurements, and the results are shown in Table S1. These characterizations demonstrate that CuNi5:5/CQDs3 wt.% has excellent chemical stability. To further improve the experiment, photocatalytic water splitting of the Cu/CQDs hybrid was measured, and the results are given in Fig. S9. A higher photocatalytic gas evolution rate for the Cu/CQDs hybrid was observed than for Cu alone, suggesting that CQDs are a key factor in the improvement of photocatalytic activity. However, the photocatalytic gas evolution rate of Cu/CQDs is still lower than that of CuNi/CQDs, indicating that Ni metal is an important component for the enhancement of photocatalytic activity.

A series of controlled experiments were carried out, and the results are shown in Table S2, demonstrating that H2 and O2 evolve in the presence of photocatalyst, solar irradiation, and water. Furthermore, both pure Ni and CQDs have no photocatalytic ability under irradiation in water. Photocatalytic water splitting of a physical mixture between CuNi5:5 bimetal and CQDs was investigated in Fig. S11, and no significant enhancement of the photocatalytic ability was observed after the addition of CQDs, confirming that no meaningful contact between CQDs and CuNi5:5 bimetal was formed in the physical mixture. In contrast, the existence of contact was confirmed with the hydrothermal method, which can enhance the photocatalytic gas evolution ability (Fig. 4b). The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rates of the photocatalysts were also investigated in the presence of a lactic acid sacrificial agent, and the results are shown in Fig. S10.

The photocatalytic activity of the CuNi5:5/CQDs3 wt.% composite has been investigated under solar irradiation; however, inevitable oxides, such as semiconductor photocatalysts, can also contribute to photocatalysis under solar irradiation. To eliminate this interference and demonstrate that photocatalysis comes from the SPR effect, wavelengths for irradiation within the SPR frequencies were used for photocatalysis. Monochromatic light with a wavelength of 600 ± 10 nm was obtained with a bandpass filter, and other experimental details remained unchanged. Plasmon resonance can be induced at this wavelength, while semiconductors have little response. CuNi5:5/CQDs3 wt.% shows a decrease of the H2 evolution rate to 8.23 μmol g−1h−1 due to a decrease in the total photon flux available for the photocatalytic water splitting. This result demonstrates that photocatalytic water splitting was driven by the SPR effect. In addition, quantum efficiency was investigated, and the results are shown in Table S3.

The hydrogen evolution rate in our system was compared with the performance of other catalysts reported in the literature, and details are shown in Table 1 . Traditional semiconductor photocatalytic materials, such as g-C3N4 and TiO2, possess relatively low rates of H2 evolution before modification, and their photocatalytic H2 evolution rates are 0.14 and 0.356 mmol g−1h−1, respectively [12,6]. After various modifications, the photocatalytic H2 evolution rates of semiconductor-based photocatalytic materials achieved significant improvement, and the highest value was 4.3 mmol g−1h−1 for MoS2/TiO2 [4–12]. Metal-based photocatalytic systems have only a few reports because metal is only a co-catalyst in most photocatalytic systems, and semiconductor still makes up the core of the catalyst. BiAg bimetal exhibits a low photocatalytic activity of 0.00012 mmol g−1h−1 [43]. Furthermore, the photocatalytic H2 evolution rates of metal–organic framework (MOF) materials are also supplied in Table 1, and lower photocatalytic H2 evolution rates than those of semiconductor-based photocatalysts were observed [44–47]. In general, the photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of CuNi/CQDs in our system is relatively low compared with that for these photocatalytic systems. However, sacrificial agents such as methanol and triethanolamine are widely available for these systems, and H2 evolution is heavily dependent on sacrificial agents. Although the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate is low in our photocatalytic system, real total water decomposition is realized. It is also worth noting that the CuNi/CQDs system has stepped away from the traditional semiconductor-driven photocatalyst field. This study provides a new and significant approach for the development of a non-semiconductor-driven photocatalytic system for effective water splitting, which could effectively broaden the scope of the photocatalytic field.

The transient photocurrent response and photoluminescence (PL) spectrum can be used as an effective way to comprehend the separation and transfer of the photogenerated electron–hole pair in the photocatalyst [44]. As shown in Fig. 5 a, pure Cu generates a small photocurrent resulting from the rapid recombination of the electron–hole pair under sunlight irradiation. After combination with Ni, CuNi5:5 shows a higher photocurrent response than pure Cu due to a synergistic effect. In comparison with pure Cu and CuNi5:5, the largest photocurrents are obtained for the CuNi5:5/CQDs3 wt.% sample, implying that the separation and transfer of charge carriers in the sample are significantly improved. In the CuNi5:5/CQDs3 wt.% sample, CQDs act as electron transfer channels that can restrict the reverse transport of the accumulated photogenerated charge carriers onto the surface of CuNi. This result was also corroborated by the photoluminescence (PL) spectra (Fig. 5b), in which fluorescence would be emitted upon electron–hole recombination. CuNi5:5/CQDs3 wt.% exhibits the lowest light emission intensity, according to the characteristic peak of plasmonic Cu, among the three samples, demonstrating that the recombination of electron–hole pairs has been substantially suppressed. Photoinduced electrons with a high fluorescence intensity mean high efficiency of electron–hole recombination, impeding photocatalytic reactions. Transient photocurrent responses and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy confirm that electron–hole separation and transfer are most efficient in the CuNi5:5/CQDs3 wt.% sample, which is consistent with the photocatalytic gas evolution rates (Fig. 4b).

The photoluminescent lifetimes of the samples were also investigated using time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy, and the results are shown in Table S4. An evident increase in the lifetime for CuNi5:5 and CuNi5:5/CQDs3 wt.% is observed, meaning that the excited-state electrons have a longer lifetime due to electron transfer between the different components [53]. This phenomenon demonstrates that the inclusion of Ni and CQDs can promote charge separation and transfer, which can enhance photocatalytic activity.

The optical absorption (Fig. 2c) and electron transfer properties (Fig. 5) demonstrate that the SPR effect and photoinduced electrons are present in the CuNi/CQDs photocatalyst upon irradiation. Based on these experimental results, a possible working mechanism for photocatalytic water splitting by CuNi/CQDs is proposed in Fig. 6 . The photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst is derived from the chemical character of the plasmon-induced electron–hole pairs in the CuNi bimetal. The plasmon resonance is excited via irradiation of the surface of the CuNi bimetal and then decays into electron–hole pairs by Landau decay, a quantum-mechanical process [48–52]. The electrons are either directly injected into adsorbed water molecules or transferred to CQDs for water splitting [8,54], and the holes in the CuNi bimetal oxidize H2O to O2 at the same time. In addition, these photoexcited electrons can reduce the Ni(OH)2 layer on the CuNi bimetal to Ni so that a partial CuNi@Ni core–shell structure is formed [55]. Water molecules are also reduced to H2 by the photoinduced electrons in the Ni shell [56]. Pure CQDs have no photocatalytic ability under sunlight in water, suggesting that few photoinduced electrons exist in the pure CQDs and that the role of CQDs is as a co-catalyst. The photoinduced electrons from CuNi can be shuttled freely to the CQDs, which serve as an electron acceptor, effectively promoting the separation of the electron–hole pairs and prolonging their lifetime.

The photocatalytic activity of the CuNi bimetal is better than that of pure Cu due to the synergistic effect between Cu and Ni, which can change the charge transfer efficiency and affect the catalytic activity. The contribution of the synergistic effect may be attributed to two aspects. (1) The interface between the CuNi bimetal and the Ni shell may play a key role and facilitate charge transfer in the photocatalytic system. It was reported that the work functions of Ni and Cu are 5.15 and 4.94 eV [57,58], and the work function of the CuNi bimetal lies at an intermediate level between Ni and Cu. When CuNi contacts Ni, electron donation from CuNi to Ni is favorable, because Ni has a higher work function than the CuNi bimetal. The CuNi@Ni core–shell structure suppresses electron–hole recombination. (2) The H+ reduction overpotential of the CuNi bimetal is lower than that of pure Cu. As shown in Fig. S12, linear scan voltammograms of the working electrodes coated with pure Cu and CuNi5:5 bimetal were performed, and pure Cu exhibits an overpotential of H+ reduction obviously higher than that of the CuNi5:5 bimetal. This result demonstrates that the higher photocatalytic activity of the CuNi bimetal may be derived from a decline in the H+ reduction overpotential. A low overpotential is beneficial to free charge transfer, enhancing the photocatalytic reduction reaction. These results for the photocatalytic water splitting rates, which are in the order Cu < CuNi < CuNi/CQDs, are consistent with the possible working mechanism, revealing the different photocatalytic abilities of different photocatalysts.

In summary, we have demonstrated that the dendritelike, plasmonic, non-semiconductor-driven photocatalyst CuNi/CQDs can be prepared by hydrothermal methods. The photocatalytic water-splitting rate of pure Cu is enhanced after alloying with Ni and modification with CQDs under irradiation. Electrons are generated from the CuNi component by Landau decay during the decay of plasmons in the CuNi/CQDs device. Hence, we propose that photocatalytic water splitting is carried out by plasmon resonance that decays into an electron–hole pair. The electron mobility of CuNi/CQDs was investigated, demonstrating that the inclusion of Ni and CQDs can promote charge separation and transfer. Moreover, sufficient stability of the photocatalyst was observed over four cycles, indicating the excellent chemical stability of the photocatalyst. This plasmonic non-semiconductor-driven photocatalyst may offer opportunities for the rational design of a new family of photocatalysts.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21571064, 21371060), the Ph.D. Start-up Fund of the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (2008A030310362), and the research fund of the Key Laboratory of Fuel Cell Technology of Guangdong Province for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
